summary: All muscles of facial expression are derived from the second pharyngeal arch and supplied by the facial nerve.
Muscles of facial expression are derived from the first pharyngeal arch and supplied by the trigeminal nerve.
Muscles of facial expression are supplied by both facial and trigeminal nerves equally.
In UMN facial palsy, the forehead is spared due to bilateral cortical representation.
In UMN facial palsy, the lower face is spared due to bilateral innervation.
In LMN facial palsy, the forehead is spared because it receives bilateral input.
The five terminal branches of the facial nerve are Temporal, Zygomatic, Buccal, Marginal mandibular, and Cervical.
The five terminal branches of the facial nerve are Temporal, Zygomatic, Buccal, Mental, and Cervical.
The five terminal branches are Frontal, Zygomatic, Buccal, Mandibular, and Cervical.
The deep facial vein connects the pterygoid plexus to the facial vein, providing a route to the cavernous sinus.
The deep facial vein connects the external jugular vein to the cavernous sinus directly.
The deep facial vein drains the parotid gland into the internal jugular vein.
Frey's syndrome occurs after parotidectomy due to aberrant regeneration of parasympathetic fibres to sweat glands.
Frey's syndrome occurs after submandibular gland removal due to sympathetic nerve damage.
Frey's syndrome is caused by facial nerve palsy after mastoid surgery.
The parotid duct (Stensen's duct) opens opposite the upper second molar tooth.
The parotid duct opens opposite the upper first premolar tooth.
The parotid duct opens into the floor of the mouth beneath the tongue.
